benchmark_description: Participate in collaborative conversations with diverse partners about grade 2 topics and texts with peers and adults in small and large groups. Follow agreed-upon rules for discussions; build on others' talk by linking comments to the remarks of others; ask for clarification and further explanation as needed.

lesson.title: Taking Turns and Building on Ideas in Discussion
lesson.objective: Students will participate in collaborative discussions by following agreed-upon rules, building on others' comments, and asking questions for clarification.
lesson.overview: When we talk with others about books, topics, or ideas, we are having a discussion. Good discussion partners listen carefully, take turns speaking, build on what others say, and ask questions when they do not understand. These rules help everyone learn and share ideas.
key_concepts: Collaborative conversation: a discussion where everyone shares ideas respectfully
Discussion rules: agreed-upon ways to take turns and listen (raise hand, look at the speaker, wait your turn)
Building on talk: connecting your idea to what someone else said (e.g., 'I agree with Maya because...' or 'That makes me think of...')
Clarifying question: a question you ask when you do not understand something (e.g., 'Can you explain what you mean?' or 'What do you mean by...?')

examples.label: Building on a classmate's comment
examples.text: Classmate: 'I think the main character was brave because she crossed the bridge alone.' | You: 'I agree with Jaylen — and I think she was also brave at the end when she stood up for her friend.'

examples.label: Asking for clarification
examples.text: Classmate: 'The story had a lot of tension.' | You: 'Can you explain what you mean by tension? I want to make sure I understand.'

common_errors: Interrupting a speaker before they are finished
Only sharing your own idea without connecting to what others said
Not asking when you do not understand

practice_questions.prompt_md: What should you do when a classmate is speaking during a discussion?

choices.label: Talk to your neighbor
choices.explanation: Talking to your neighbor while someone is speaking is disrespectful and distracting.

choices.label: Listen carefully and look at the speaker
choices.explanation: Good discussion partners listen carefully and give eye contact to the speaker.

choices.label: Start writing your own idea
choices.explanation: Writing during someone's turn means you are not listening — wait until it is your turn.

choices.label: Raise your hand and interrupt
choices.explanation: Raising your hand is correct, but you should wait — not interrupt — until the speaker is done.

practice_questions.explanation_md: **Active listening** — looking at the speaker and focusing on their words — is the first rule of a good collaborative discussion.

choices.label: Shouting your answer before the teacher finishes the question
choices.explanation: Shouting out breaks discussion rules — wait to be called on.

choices.label: Raising your hand and waiting to be called on
choices.explanation: Raising your hand and waiting your turn follows agreed-upon discussion rules.

choices.label: Telling your neighbor the answer while someone else is talking
choices.explanation: Side conversations during discussion break the rules — wait for your turn.

choices.label: Leaving the discussion when you disagree
choices.explanation: Leaving is not a discussion rule — staying and respectfully sharing your view is.

practice_questions.explanation_md: Discussion rules include raising your hand, waiting to be called on, and listening while others speak. These help the whole group share ideas fairly.

practice_questions.prompt_md:
A student says: *'I agree with Marcus, and I think the wolf was also mean to the pigs because he scared them away from their homes.'*

What is this student doing?

choices.label: Interrupting Marcus
choices.explanation: The student is not interrupting — they referenced Marcus's comment and added to it.

choices.label: Building on Marcus's comment by linking their idea to what he said
choices.explanation: Starting with 'I agree with Marcus' shows the student listened and is building on his comment.

choices.label: Asking Marcus a clarifying question
choices.explanation: The student is adding an idea, not asking a question.

choices.label: Changing the topic of the discussion
choices.explanation: The student is staying on the same topic (the wolf) and adding a new detail.

practice_questions.explanation_md: **Building on talk** means connecting your idea to what a classmate said. Phrases like 'I agree with ___' or 'That makes me think of...' show you are listening and adding to the discussion.

practice_questions.prompt_md: Your classmate says, 'The text had a lot of implicit meaning.' You do not understand the words 'implicit meaning.' What should you do?

choices.label: Pretend you understand and say nothing
choices.explanation: Pretending to understand does not help you learn — ask for clarification.

choices.label: Ask your classmate to explain what 'implicit meaning' means
choices.explanation: Asking for clarification helps you understand and shows you are engaged in the discussion.

choices.label: Change the topic so you do not have to think about it
choices.explanation: Changing the topic avoids the issue instead of seeking understanding.

choices.label: Tell the teacher your classmate used a wrong word
choices.explanation: Asking for clarification is more productive than telling the teacher the classmate was wrong.

practice_questions.explanation_md: Asking a **clarifying question** (like 'Can you explain what you mean?') shows you are listening and helps you understand. It is a good discussion skill.

practice_questions.prompt_md:
Which response BEST builds on a classmate's idea?

Classmate said: *'I think the author wrote this book to teach us about being kind.'*

choices.label: My favorite color is blue.
choices.explanation: This does not connect to the classmate's idea at all.

choices.label: I agree — and the scene where Maria shares her lunch shows that message best.
choices.explanation: This builds on the classmate's idea by agreeing AND adding specific text evidence to support the point.

choices.label: I already knew that.
choices.explanation: This does not add any new idea or connection to the discussion.

choices.label: That's wrong — the book is about sharing.
choices.explanation: Sharing IS being kind — this response contradicts without adding to the idea. Respectful disagreement would acknowledge the classmate's idea first.

practice_questions.explanation_md: The strongest discussion responses **agree or disagree respectfully** and add **specific evidence** or reasoning that connects to what the previous speaker said.

practice_questions.prompt_md: Which sentence is a **clarifying question** someone might ask in a book discussion?

choices.label: I liked the book.
choices.explanation: This is a statement of opinion, not a clarifying question.

choices.label: What do you mean when you say the ending was surprising?
choices.explanation: This is a clarifying question — it asks the speaker to explain their idea more fully.

choices.label: The main character is named Sofia.
choices.explanation: This is a statement of fact, not a clarifying question.

choices.label: Let me add to that by saying the author uses vivid imagery.
choices.explanation: This is building on talk — adding to an idea — not asking a clarifying question.

practice_questions.explanation_md: A **clarifying question** asks someone to explain or expand on what they said. It usually starts with 'What do you mean by...?' or 'Can you say more about...?'

practice_questions.prompt_md: A group is discussing their science reading about weather. Leo talks for five minutes without stopping. What should Leo do to be a better discussion partner?

choices.label: Talk even longer so he can share all his ideas
choices.explanation: Monopolizing the conversation is not a discussion rule — everyone deserves a turn.

choices.label: Pause, invite others to respond, and take turns speaking
choices.explanation: Good discussion partners give others a chance to speak by pausing, asking questions, and not dominating the conversation.

choices.label: Stop talking completely for the rest of class
choices.explanation: Participation is important — Leo should share, but leave room for others too.

choices.label: Only speak when the teacher calls on him
choices.explanation: Collaborative discussions involve everyone — not just teacher-directed turns — but sharing should be balanced.

practice_questions.explanation_md: Good discussion partners **share the floor** — they speak and then pause so others can respond. Balanced participation helps everyone contribute.

practice_questions.prompt_md: During a discussion about a story, your classmate says something you disagree with. What is the BEST way to respond?

choices.label: Say 'That's totally wrong!' loudly.
choices.explanation: This is disrespectful — disagreement should be expressed calmly and with reasons.

choices.label: Stay quiet and say nothing so no conflict starts.
choices.explanation: Staying silent when you disagree misses a chance to contribute your perspective respectfully.

choices.label: Say 'I see it differently — I think... because...'
choices.explanation: This respectfully signals disagreement and explains your reasoning — a strong discussion move.

choices.label: Interrupt your classmate before they finish.
choices.explanation: Interrupting is not respectful — wait for them to finish before responding.

practice_questions.explanation_md: Respectful disagreement uses phrases like **'I see it differently'** or **'I'm not sure I agree because...'** followed by reasoning. This keeps the discussion productive.

practice_questions.prompt_md:
Read this exchange:

Student A: 'The author repeated the phrase "stay brave" to remind us of the theme.'
Student B: 'Building on what you said — I noticed the author used that phrase three times, always when the character faced a hard choice.'

What is Student B doing?

choices.label: Changing the subject
choices.explanation: Student B is on the same subject — the phrase and the theme.

choices.label: Building on Student A's comment with specific text evidence
choices.explanation: Student B connects to Student A's point ('Building on what you said') and adds specific evidence (three times, hard choices) — strong discussion technique.

choices.label: Asking a clarifying question
choices.explanation: Student B is adding information, not asking a question.

choices.label: Disagreeing with Student A
choices.explanation: Student B is agreeing and extending Student A's idea, not disagreeing.

practice_questions.explanation_md: **Building on talk** means acknowledging what someone said and adding new supporting detail. Using the phrase 'Building on what you said...' is a strong discussion move.

practice_questions.prompt_md: Which discussion behavior shows ALL three skills: following rules, building on talk, and asking for clarification?

choices.label: Shouting an answer, repeating what a classmate said, and asking a question loudly
choices.explanation: Shouting breaks discussion rules — good discussion involves patient, respectful participation.

choices.label: Raising your hand, saying 'I agree with Jade and want to add...' then asking 'Can you explain what you meant by interesting setting?'
choices.explanation: Raising hand = following rules. 'I agree with Jade...' = building on talk. 'Can you explain...?' = clarifying question. All three skills shown.

choices.label: Listening quietly, saying 'I agree,' and never asking anything
choices.explanation: This shows listening and some agreement, but lacks building on talk and asking for clarification.

choices.label: Speaking first without waiting, adding a new topic, then asking an off-topic question
choices.explanation: Speaking without waiting breaks rules, adding a new topic doesn't build on talk, and an off-topic question is not a clarifying question.

practice_questions.explanation_md: Strong discussion partners **follow rules** (raise hand, wait), **build on talk** (connect their ideas to others'), and **ask clarifying questions** when they need more explanation.

mastery_check.prompt_md: Which phrase is the BEST way to build on a classmate's comment?

choices.label: Whatever.
choices.explanation: This is dismissive and does not engage with the classmate's idea.

choices.label: That's what I was going to say.
choices.explanation: This does not add anything new or connect specifically to the classmate's comment.

choices.label: Adding to what Priya said, I also think the character changed because of the storm scene.
choices.explanation: This names the classmate, builds on their point, and adds a new specific idea — all hallmarks of strong discussion talk.

choices.label: My turn now.
choices.explanation: This only claims a turn without connecting to or building on anyone's ideas.

mastery_check.explanation_md: To **build on talk**, reference the classmate by name, connect to their idea, and add a new thought or piece of evidence.

mastery_check.prompt_md: When is the BEST time to ask a clarifying question?

choices.label: Before anyone has spoken
choices.explanation: There is nothing to clarify before anyone has spoken.

choices.label: When you do not understand something a classmate said
choices.explanation: Ask a clarifying question when you need a speaker to explain or expand on what they said.

choices.label: To interrupt a classmate mid-sentence
choices.explanation: Interrupting is not a good discussion rule — wait until the speaker is finished.

choices.label: Only when the teacher tells you to ask one
choices.explanation: Clarifying questions come naturally from the discussion — you do not need teacher permission to ask one.

mastery_check.explanation_md: Ask a **clarifying question** whenever you are unsure what a speaker meant. This shows you are listening and helps the whole group understand.

mastery_check.prompt_md:
A small group is discussing a story. Read what happens:

- Kenji interrupts before others finish.
- He does not reference what anyone said.
- He never asks questions.

Which description BEST fits Kenji's discussion behavior?

choices.label: He is following all discussion rules.
choices.explanation: Kenji is breaking rules (interrupting), not building on talk, and not asking questions — he is NOT following discussion rules.

choices.label: He is building on talk and asking clarifying questions.
choices.explanation: He is not building on others' comments and is not asking questions.

choices.label: He is not following discussion rules, not building on talk, and not asking clarifying questions.
choices.explanation: Kenji breaks all three skills: he interrupts (rules), ignores others' comments (building on talk), and asks no questions (clarification).

choices.label: He is an excellent discussion partner.
choices.explanation: An excellent discussion partner listens, takes turns, builds on talk, and asks questions — Kenji does none of these.

mastery_check.explanation_md: Strong discussion partners follow rules, build on what others say, and ask clarifying questions. Kenji is not demonstrating any of these skills.

remediation.prompt_md: What should you do when it is someone else's turn to speak?

choices.label: Listen carefully
choices.explanation: When it is someone else's turn, listen carefully so you understand their idea.

choices.label: Talk at the same time
choices.explanation: Talking at the same time is interrupting — wait for your turn.

choices.label: Think about something else
choices.explanation: Thinking about other things means you are not listening — you need to focus on the speaker.

choices.label: Walk away
choices.explanation: Leaving the discussion is not respectful or productive.

remediation.explanation_md: When it is someone else's turn to speak, **listen carefully**. This helps you understand their idea so you can build on it when it is your turn.

remediation.prompt_md: Which is a good discussion rule?

choices.label: Talk whenever you want
choices.explanation: Taking turns is a discussion rule — talking whenever you want interrupts others.

choices.label: Raise your hand and wait to be called on
choices.explanation: Raising your hand and waiting is a key discussion rule — it makes sure everyone gets a turn.

choices.label: Only the loudest voice wins
choices.explanation: Volume is not how discussion works — everyone's ideas matter.

choices.label: Only the teacher can talk
choices.explanation: Collaborative discussions include all students sharing ideas, not just the teacher.

remediation.explanation_md: Discussion rules like **raising your hand and waiting your turn** make sure everyone gets a chance to share ideas.

remediation.prompt_md: A classmate says something you do not understand. What should you do?

choices.label: Ask them to explain what they mean
choices.explanation: Asking for clarification helps you understand — it is a sign of a good listener.

choices.label: Pretend you understand and say 'okay'
choices.explanation: Pretending does not help you learn. It is better to ask.

choices.label: Tell them they are wrong
choices.explanation: You may not understand yet — ask for clarification before deciding if you disagree.

choices.label: Stop listening and think about lunch
choices.explanation: Tuning out means you will miss important ideas — keep listening and ask if confused.

remediation.explanation_md: When you do not understand, ask a **clarifying question** like 'Can you explain what you mean?' — this helps you and everyone in the discussion.

remediation.prompt_md: Which sentence BEST builds on a classmate's idea?

choices.label: I want to talk about something different.
choices.explanation: Changing topics does not build on the classmate's idea.

choices.label: I agree with Zara, and I also think the dog was the real hero.
choices.explanation: This names Zara, agrees, and adds a new idea — it builds on Zara's comment.

choices.label: That is a weird thing to say.
choices.explanation: This is dismissive and does not add to the discussion.

choices.label: My lunch today was good.
choices.explanation: This is completely off-topic.

remediation.explanation_md: To **build on a classmate's idea**, name them, connect to their point, and add something new. 'I agree with ___ and I also think...' is a great discussion starter.

remediation.prompt_md: You want to add an idea to the discussion. What should you do FIRST?

choices.label: Start talking immediately
choices.explanation: Starting without waiting may interrupt someone — raise your hand first.

choices.label: Raise your hand and wait to be called on
choices.explanation: Raising your hand shows you want to talk and waiting shows respect for the speaker.

choices.label: Write your idea down and show no one
choices.explanation: Keeping your idea to yourself does not contribute to the discussion.

choices.label: Tell the person next to you first
choices.explanation: Side conversations during discussion are distracting — share your idea with the whole group.

remediation.explanation_md: To share in a discussion, **raise your hand** and wait to be called on. Then connect your idea to what others have said.
